summary: Paul Graham argues that his fear of having kids was based on a biased, negative sample of parenthood and a misunderstanding of what daily life with children is actually like. After becoming a parent, he found that the experience brings frequent moments of peace, friendship, and happiness that outweigh the real costs in time, freedom, and ambition.
key_points: His pre‑kids view was skewed by noticing parents only in stressful public moments.
Parenthood brings frequent, ordinary moments of deep contentment that are hard to describe.
Kids reduce productivity and constrain schedules, but meaningful work can still happen.
Having kids can lower personal ambition because attention shifts toward them.
Much of the freedom he valued before kids was unused and came with loneliness.
money_quotes: Something I dreaded turned out to be wonderful.

People don't talk about these much — the magic is hard to put into words, and all other parents know about them anyway — but one of the great things about having kids is that there are so many times when you feel there is nowhere else you'd rather be, and nothing else you'd rather be doing.
You don't just love them. They become your friends too.
But you can't let work spill promiscuously through your whole life, like I used to before I had kids.
I had plenty of happy times before I had kids. But if I count up happy moments, not just potential happiness but actual happy moments, there are more after kids than before.
key_insight: Parenthood trades some freedom and productivity for a steady stream of everyday joy and meaning that he hadn’t understood from the outside.
